summary:
Chili peppers (Capsicum spp.) were among the earliest domesticated plants in the Americas, with evidence for their cultivation in Mesoamerica and northern South America dating to approximately 7500–6000 BCE. Starch grains attributed to Capsicum have been recovered from grinding stones at sites in Mexico and Ecuador, suggesting that peppers were being processed and consumed as part of daily subsistence well before their morphological domestication was complete.

Chili peppers were valued not only as a food flavoring but also for their preservative properties and possible medicinal uses. They were among the first New World crops to spread globally after European contact in the late 15th century CE, transforming cuisines across Asia, Africa, and Europe. The deep antiquity of their use reflects the sophistication of early American plant management and the broad ecological knowledge that prehistoric communities brought to the domestication process.
